---
author: jshn9515
date: 2026-03-19
date-modified: 2026-05-23
open-graph:
  title: ch2.2-autograd-contexts
  description: "2.2 Gradient Modes in PyTorch: Controlling How Computation Graphs Are Recorded"
title: "2.2 Gradient Modes in PyTorch: Controlling Computation Graph Recording"
order: 2
---

The previous section showed how PyTorch records operations for automatic differentiation. In practice, not every computation should be recorded.

Training needs graph recording because we later call `backward()`. Validation and inference usually only need outputs, so recording the graph wastes memory and time.

This section separates tensor eligibility (`requires_grad`) from context-level gradient recording (`no_grad`, `enable_grad`, `set_grad_enabled`, and `inference_mode`).

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

print('PyTorch version:', torch.__version__)
```

## 2.2.1 By Default, PyTorch Records What It Needs

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```{python}
model = nn.Linear(6, 4)
x = torch.randn(10, 6)
y = torch.randn(10, 4)

y_pred = model(x)
print('y_pred.requires_grad:', y_pred.requires_grad)
print('y_pred.grad_fn:', y_pred.grad_fn.name())
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```{python}
for name, param in model.named_parameters():
    print(f'{name}.requires_grad: {param.requires_grad}')
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```{python}
loss = F.mse_loss(y_pred, y)
loss.backward()

assert model.weight.grad is not None
assert model.bias.grad is not None
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

## 2.2.2 no_grad(): Do Not Record This Block

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
with torch.no_grad():
    y_pred = model(x)

print('y_pred.requires_grad:', y_pred.requires_grad)
print('y_pred.grad_fn:', y_pred.grad_fn)
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```{python}
loss = F.mse_loss(y_pred, y)
print('loss.requires_grad:', loss.requires_grad)

try:
    loss.backward()
except RuntimeError as err:
    print('RuntimeError:', err)
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```python
model.eval()

with torch.no_grad():
    for X, y in valid_loader:
        y_pred = model(X)
        loss = loss_fn(y_pred, y)
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```{python}
x = torch.randn(10, 6, requires_grad=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    print('x.requires_grad:', x.requires_grad)
    z = x.sin()
    print('z.requires_grad:', z.requires_grad)
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```{python}
x = torch.randn(10, 6, requires_grad=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    a = x.sin()

b = x.cos()

print('a.requires_grad:', a.requires_grad)
print('b.requires_grad:', b.requires_grad)
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
x = torch.randn(10, 6, requires_grad=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    a = x.sin()

print('a.requires_grad:', a.requires_grad)
a.requires_grad_()
print('a.requires_grad:', a.requires_grad)
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

## 2.2.3 enable_grad(): Re-enable Recording Locally

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```{python}
x = torch.randn(4, requires_grad=True)

with torch.no_grad():
    a = x.sin()
    print('a.requires_grad:', a.requires_grad)

    with torch.enable_grad():
        b = x.cos()
        print('b.requires_grad:', b.requires_grad)

    c = x.tan()
    print('c.requires_grad:', c.requires_grad)
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```{python}
is_training = False
x = torch.randn(10, 6)

with torch.set_grad_enabled(is_training):
    y_pred = model(x)

print('y_pred.requires_grad:', y_pred.requires_grad)
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```python
def run_one_epoch(model: Module, dataloader: DataLoader, training: bool):
    model.train(training)

    with torch.set_grad_enabled(training):
        for X, y in dataloader:
            y_pred = model(X)
            loss = loss_fn(y_pred, y)

            if training:
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                loss.backward()
                optimizer.step()
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

## 2.2.4 inference_mode(): Pure Inference

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

[^1]: `torch.inference_mode()` was introduced in PyTorch 1.9 as an inference-time performance optimization. For implementation details, see [RFC-0011-InferenceMode](https://github.com/pytorch/rfcs/blob/master/RFC-0011-InferenceMode.md).

```{python}
with torch.no_grad():
    x = torch.randn(4)

x.requires_grad_()
y = x.dot(x)
y.backward()

print('x.grad:', x.grad)
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```{python}
with torch.inference_mode():
    x = torch.randn(4)

try:
    x.requires_grad_()
except RuntimeError as err:
    print('RuntimeError:', err)
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

## 2.2.5 How to Choose Among the Modes

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

## 2.2.6 Summary

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.
